13 Plotting and Optimization in Microeconomic Models 13.1 Basic Contour Plot 13.2 Economic Example: Production Isoquants and Isocosts 13.3 Utility Contour and Gradient Vectors

13 Plotting and Optimization in Microeconomic Models Plotting helps us visualize economic relationships before solving optimization problems. In economics, plots allow us to see: how functions behave where optimum points occur the geometry of optimization (tangencies and curvature) Many economic models involve two variables, so contour plot are useful. 13.1 Basic Contour Plot \(f(x,y) = x^2+y^2\) Each contour line represents \(f(x,y)=c\) which are circles around the origin. 13.1.0.1 Define the function rm(list=ls()) f<-\(x,y) x^2+y^2 R can now evaluate the function for different values of \(x\) and \(y\) 13.1.0.2 Create values for axes x_vals<-seq(-5,5, length.out=100) y_vals<-seq(-5,5, length.out=100) seq() generates values for x and y axes 13.1.0.3 Evaluate the function on a grid Z <- outer(x_vals, y_vals, Vectorize(f)) outer() computes the function value for every combination of \(x\) and \(y\) The result is a matrix of values required for contour plots. 13.1.0.4 Plot contour lines contour(x_vals, y_vals, Z, col="blue", lwd=2, xlab="x", ylab="y", main="Contour Plot of f(x,y)") Each curve represents a level set \(f(x,y) = constant\) Contour plots are useful for economic models with two decision variables. 13.2 Economic Example: Production Isoquants and Isocosts Production Function \(Q(K,L) = K^{0.5}L^{0.5}\) where K=capital and L=labor An isoquant represents combinations of inputs producing the same output An isocost line shows all combinations of capital and labor that have the same total cost \(C=rK+wL\) where K=capital, L=labor, r=rental rate of capital, w=wage rate The firm minimizes cost by choosing the input bundle where: \(\frac{MP_L}{MP_K} = \frac{w}{r}\) This occurs where the isoquant is tangent to the isocost line 13.2.0.1 Define the function rm(list=ls()) Q <- \(K,L) K^0.5 * L^0.5 This function computes output for any combination of capital and labor. We need this because isoquants represent combinations that yield the same value of Q. 13.2.0.2 Create input grids K_vals <- seq(1,20,length.out=100) L_vals <- seq(1,20,length.out=100) These represent possible capital and labor combinations 13.2.0.3 Compute output levels Z <- outer(K_vals, L_vals, Vectorize(Q)) Each element of Z represents \(Q(K,L)\) for a specific combination of inputs 13.2.0.4 Choose input prices and cost Suppose: \(r=2\) \(w=4\) \(C=40\) r <- 2 w <- 4 C <- 40 These represent the prices of inputs and firm’s budget for inputs. 13.2.0.5 Derive the Isocost Line From \(C=rK+wL\) solve for \(L\) \(L=\frac{C-rK}{w}\) isocost <- (C - r*K_vals)/w For each value of capital, we compute the labor level that keeps cost constant. This generates the isocost line. 13.2.0.6 Compute the Optimal Input Bundle \(Q=K^{0.5}L^{0.5}\) The tangency condition gives \(\frac{L}{K} = \frac{r}{w}\) library(nleqslv) foc_system <- function(x) { K <- x[1] L <- x[2] c( L / K - r / w, r * K + w * L - C ) } sol <- nleqslv(c(8, 6), foc_system) K_star <- sol$x[1] L_star <- sol$x[2] K_star ## [1] 10 L_star ## [1] 5 foc_system() defines the two equations we want to solve simultaneously. The first equation is the tangency condition. the second equation is the isocost constraint. nleqslv(c(8,6), foc_system) starts from an initial guess (8,6) and solves for K and L. Using nleqslv is helpful because you will see that the optimum comes from solving a system of economic conditions, not from plugging numbers into a shortcut. It is especially useful when: the production function is more complicated, the tangency condition is not easy to solve by hand, or you want a consistent numerical method across topics. 13.2.0.7 Plotting contour(K_vals, L_vals, Z, levels = c(5, 8, 10), col = c("blue", "darkgreen", "purple"), lwd = 2, xlab = "Capital (K)", ylab = "Labor (L)", main = "Isoquant and Isocost") lines(K_vals, isocost, col = "red", lwd = 2) points(K_star, L_star, pch = 19, col = "black", cex = 1.3) text(K_star + 0.5, L_star, "Optimum", col = "black") One more improvement: if you want the graph to show the actual tangency isoquant, set the contour level equal to the output at the optimum: Q_star <- Q(K_star, L_star) contour(K_vals, L_vals, Z, levels = c(Q_star), col = "blue", lwd = 2, xlab = "Capital (K)", ylab = "Labor (L)", main = "Isoquant and Isocost with Optimum") lines(K_vals, isocost, col = "red", lwd = 2) points(K_star, L_star, pch = 19, col = "black", cex = 1.3) text(K_star + 0.5, L_star, "Optimum", col = "black") The blue curve is the isoquant, every point on that curve produces the same output level. The curve slopes downward because inputs can substitute for each other. The red line is the isocost. It shows all combinations of capital and labor that cost the same amount. The slope is the price ratio of inputs. So, along the red line, the firm spends the same total cost. The black point labeled Optimumis where the isoquant and isocost touch each other (tangency). This is the cost-minimizing input bundle. The numbers, \(K^*=10; L^*=5\) is the combination that produces the output at the lowest possible cost given input prices. 13.2.0.8 Why the Tangency Matters If the firm moved: Above the isocost Production is possible but cost is higher Inside the isoquant Cost is lower but output is insufficient The tangency point is the only bundle that produces the required output at minimum cost. 13.2.0.8.1 Graph looks strange Since the Capital range goes to 20 and labor goes to 15, let’s make the tangency clearer. valid<-isocost>=0 # Output level at optimum Q_star <- Q(K_star,L_star) # Plot isoquant contour(K_vals, L_vals, Z, levels=c(Q_star), col="blue", lwd=3, xlab="Capital (K)", ylab="Labor (L)", xlim=c(0,15), ylim=c(0,10), main="Isoquant and Isocost with Optimum") # Add isocost lines(K_vals[valid], isocost[valid], col="red", lwd=2) # Add optimum point points(K_star, L_star, pch=19, col="black", cex=1.4) # Arrow to optimum arrows(K_star+1.5, L_star+1, K_star, L_star, length=0.1) # Label optimum text(K_star+1.7, L_star+1, paste0("Optimum (", round(K_star,1), ", ", round(L_star,1), ")"), col="black") 13.3 Utility Contour and Gradient Vectors This helps visualize consumer preferences before solving the optimization problem. We will: Define a utility function Plot indifference curves (contours) Compute gradient vectors (marginal utilities) Overlay both on the same graph 13.3.0.1 Define the function \(U(x,y) = Ax^{\alpha}+By^{\beta}\) where A and B are importance weights for goods x and y, \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are curvature parameters that determine how utility changes as consumption increases. rm(list=ls()) A<-6 B<-4 alpha<-0.8 beta<-1.2 U<-\(x,y){A*x^alpha+B*y^beta} 13.3.0.2 Create gird of consumption bundles x_vals <- seq(1, 10, length.out = 100) y_vals <- seq(1, 10, length.out = 100) 13.3.0.3 Evaluate utility for all bundles Z <- outer(x_vals, y_vals, Vectorize(U)) This creates a matrix for creating a contour plot of the combinations of x and y. 13.3.0.4 Gradient (Marginal Utilities) The gradient shows how utility changes when consumption increases. Mathematically: \(\nabla U(x,y) = (\frac{\partial U}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial U}{\partial y})\) These are marginal utilities. 13.3.0.5 Compute Gradient Values on a Grid xg <- seq(1, 10, by = 1.5) yg <- seq(1, 10, by = 1.5) grid <- expand.grid(x = xg, y = yg) grad_x <- A * alpha * grid$x^(alpha - 1) grad_y <- B * beta * grid$y^(beta - 1) Why is there alpha - 1 and beta-1? This comes from the power rule…when differentiating \(x^{\alpha}\) expand.grid() creates coordinate pairs like (1,1) (1, 2.5) .... These show the direction utility increases fastest. 13.3.0.6 Rescale Gradient Arrows Raw gradients can be very large or very small. To make the plot readable, we scale them. arrow_len <- 0.4 norm <- sqrt(grad_x^2 + grad_y^2) ux <- arrow_len * grad_x / norm uy <- arrow_len * grad_y / norm arrow_len sets the length we want the arrows to have on the plot. So every arrow will be scaled so its length is about 0.4 units. Why we do this? If we plotted raw gradients, arrows might be: extremely long near large values extremely short near small values This would make the graph messy or unreadable. So we control arrow length manually. You can use closer to 0 for shorter arrows and 1 and more for longer arrows. It affects the appearance of the arrows. norm <- sqrt(grad_x^2 + grad_y^2) This computed the length of each gradient vector since we want to normalize the arrows. Normalization means: keep the direction control the size To do that we must know the original vector length. Do we need the ^2? Yes. The squares come from the Euclidean distance formula. Without squaring we would not get the correct vector length. Example: Vector = (3,4) True length: \(\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5\) If we did not square: \(\sqrt{3+4}\) that would be wrong. So the squares are mathematically required. ux <- arrow_len * grad_x / norm creates the horizontal component of the scaled arrow. That makes the vector length equal to 1. This is called normalizing the vector. Now the vector shows only direction, not magnitude. Multiplying by arrow_len sets the final arrow size. So every arrow has the same length. The same is happening with uy 13.3.0.7 Plot Contours and Gradient Arrows contour(x_vals, y_vals, Z, nlevels = 8, col = "blue", lwd = 2, xlab = "Good x", ylab = "Good y", main = "Utility Contours and Gradient Vectors") arrows(grid$x, grid$y, grid$x + ux, grid$y + uy, length = 0.08, col = "red") Each contour represents an indifference curve. All points on that curve give the same level of satisfaction. The arrows represent the gradient of utility. They show the direction of steepest increase in utility. The arrows point up and to the right because it’s moving toward larger bundles, increasing satisfaction. Always remember: The gradient vector is always perpendicular to the contour line. That is why the arrows cross the blue curves at right angles. So what does it tell you? Utility increases northeast, meaning higher satisfaction occurs with more goods. Preferences are smooth, meaning small changes in consumption change utility gradually. Arrows look similar everywhere because exponents are close to 1. If exponents were very different, the arrows would not look that similar. 13.3.1 Let’s Add Budget Line \(p_xx+p_yy = M\) where x and y are quantities of good x and y, \(p_x\) and \(p_y\) are prices of goods x and y \(M\) is income. The consumer wants the highest possible utility without spending more than income. \(p_x = 1; p_y = 1; M=10\) px <- 1 py <- 1 M <- 10 budget_y <- (M - px * x_vals) / py valid_budget <- budget_y >= 0 This computes the budget line. For every value of \(x\), R calculates the corresponding value of y that exactly uses all income. We only keep the part of the line where y is nonnegative 13.3.1.1 Plot contour(x_vals, y_vals, Z, nlevels = 8, col = "blue", lwd = 2, xlab = "Good x", ylab = "Good y", main = "Utility Contours, Gradient Vectors, and Budget Line") arrows(grid$x, grid$y, grid$x + ux, grid$y + uy, length = 0.08, col = "red") lines(x_vals[valid_budget], budget_y[valid_budget], col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2) 13.3.1.2 Solve the optimal bundle library(nleqslv) system_eq <- function(z){ x <- z[1] y <- z[2] c( (A*alpha*x^(alpha-1))/(B*beta*y^(beta-1)) - px/py, px*x + py*y - M ) } sol <- nleqslv(c(4,6), system_eq) x_star <- sol$x[1] y_star <- sol$x[2] x_star ## [1] 0.1010205 y_star ## [1] 9.898979 13.3.1.3 Add the optimum to the plot contour(x_vals, y_vals, Z, nlevels = 8, col = "blue", lwd = 2, xlab = "Good x", ylab = "Good y", main = "Utility Contours, Gradient Vectors, and Budget Line") arrows(grid$x, grid$y, grid$x + ux, grid$y + uy, length = 0.08, col = "red") lines(x_vals[valid_budget], budget_y[valid_budget], col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2) points(x_star, y_star, pch = 19, col = "black", cex = 1.4) text(x_star + 0.3, y_star, paste0("Optimum (", round(x_star,2), ", ", round(y_star,2), ")"), pos = 4) What this shows is that the consumer buys mostly one good.

14 Returns to Scale 14.1 Production Function We use the Cobb-Douglas \(Q=K^{\alpha}L^{(1-\alpha)}\) 14.1.0.1 Define the function rm(list=ls()) alpha<-0.4 Q<-\(K,L){K^alpha*L^(1-alpha)} 14.1.0.2 We have starting input bundle We need an initial amount of capital and labor. \(K=8; L=12\) K<-8 L<-12 14.1.0.3 Choose scaling factors We need to scale both inputs by: \(t=0.25, 0.75, 1, 3\) t_vals<-c(0.25, 0.75, 1,3) Each value of \(t\) tells us how much to multiply both \(K\) and \(L\): t = 0.25 quarter both inputs t=1 keep inputs unchanged t=3 increase both inputs triple times 14.1.0.4 Compute \(Q(tK,tL)\) scaled_output <- sapply(t_vals, function(t) Q(t * K, t * L)) For each value of \(t\), R computes: \(Q(tK,tL)\) So it calculates output after scaling both inputs by the same proportion. sapply() applies the same calculation to each value of t_vals. 14.1.0.5 Compare with \(tQ(K,L)\) To determine returns to scale, we compare: \(Q(tK, tL)\) with \(tQ(K,L)\) Compute the original output base_output <- Q(K, L) Compute the proportional benchmark: proportional_output <- t_vals * base_output base_output is the output at the original input bundle. proportional_output is what output would be if it changed exactly in proportion to the input scaling. This comparison tells us the type of returns to scale. 14.1.0.6 Results results <- data.frame( t = t_vals, Q_tK_tL = scaled_output, t_times_QKL = proportional_output ) results ## t Q_tK_tL t_times_QKL ## 1 0.25 2.550849 2.550849 ## 2 0.75 7.652547 7.652547 ## 3 1.00 10.203396 10.203396 ## 4 3.00 30.610188 30.610188 This creates a table with: the scaling factor  actual scaled output \(Q(tK,tL)\) proportional benchmark \(tQ(K,L)\) This makes it easy to compare the two. 14.1.0.7 Determine the type of returns to scale Rule If \(Q(tK,tL)>tQ(K,L)\) then returns to scale are increasing If \(Q(tK,tL) = tQ(K,L)\) then returns to scale are constant If \(Q(tK,tL)<tQ(K,L)\) then returns to scale are decreasing. For this example, we expect constant returns to scale because exponents add up to 1. 14.1.0.8 Verify all.equal(scaled_output, proportional_output) ## [1] TRUE 14.1.1 Plotting the comparison plot(t_vals, scaled_output, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "blue", lwd = 2, xlab = "Scaling factor (t)", ylab = "Output", main = "Returns to Scale") lines(t_vals, proportional_output, type = "b", pch = 17, col = "red", lwd = 2) legend("topleft", legend = c("Q(tK, tL)", "t Q(K, L)"), col = c("blue", "red"), pch = c(19, 17), lwd = 2) You don’t really see but the lines overlap because returns to scale are constant. 14.2 Exercise March 10: Isoquant and Isocost Visualization Suppose a firm has the production function \(Q(K,L)=K^{0.6}L^{0.3}\) input prices and total cost are: \(r=3, w=2, C=30\) Tasks: Define the function Create grid values for capital and labor from 1 to 15 Compute output levels Plot isoquants Compute isocost line Add isocost line to the graph Solve for the optimal bundle using the tangency condition and nleqslv Add the optimal bundle to the graph Explain why the tangency point represents the cost-minimizing input combination. 15 Part I. Elasticity: Calculations, “What If” Changes, and Visualization 15.1 What Elasticity Means 15.2 Elasticity When a Demand Curve Is Given 15.3 Code: Plotting the Demand Curve and Elasticity 15.4 Deep Code Explanation 15.5 “What If?” Elasticity Changes When the Demand Curve Changes 15.6 Elasticity When a Supply Curve Is Given

15 Part I. Elasticity: Calculations, “What If” Changes, and Visualization 15.1 What Elasticity Means Elasticity measures responsiveness. In economics, the most common elasticity is price elasticity of demand, which tells us: If price changes, by how much does quantity demanded respond? The standard formula is: \[E_d = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}\] For supply: \[E_s = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}\] The formula looks the same, but the interpretation differs: For demand, elasticity is usually negative because higher price lowers quantity demanded. For supply, elasticity is usually positive because higher price raises quantity supplied. 15.2 Elasticity When a Demand Curve Is Given Suppose demand is: \[Q = 100 - 5P\] This is a linear demand curve. What the equation means economically: The intercept is 100, so if price were zero, quantity demanded would be 100. The slope is \(-5\), so every 1-unit increase in price lowers quantity demanded by 5 units. Key economics lesson: A constant slope does not mean constant elasticity. 15.3 Code: Plotting the Demand Curve and Elasticity P <- seq(1, 15, by = 0.5) Q <- 100 - 5 * P elasticity <- (-5) * (P / Q) plot(P, Q, type = "l", lwd = 2, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Quantity Demanded", main = "Linear Demand Curve") plot(P, elasticity, type = "l", lwd = 2, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Price Elasticity of Demand", main = "Elasticity Along the Demand Curve") 15.4 Deep Code Explanation Line 1: P <- seq(1, 15, by = 0.5) Creates a vector of price values: \(1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, \ldots, 15\). A graph needs many values to look smooth. Economically, we are asking: What would quantity demanded be at many different prices? Line 2: Q <- 100 - 5 * P Computes quantity demanded for every value of P. R is vectorized — it applies the formula to the whole vector at once. This turns the demand equation into actual economic outcomes and demonstrates the law of demand. Line 3: elasticity <- (-5) * (P / Q) Computes the elasticity formula at every point along the demand curve. For \(Q = 100 - 5P\), we have \(dQ/dP = -5\), so: \[E_d = -5 \cdot \frac{P}{Q}\] Even though \(dQ/dP = -5\) is constant, the ratio \(P/Q\) changes as price changes — that is why elasticity varies along the curve. Economics meaning: - At low prices and high quantities, demand tends to be less elastic. - At high prices and low quantities, demand tends to be more elastic. 15.5 “What If?” Elasticity Changes When the Demand Curve Changes 15.5.1 Example A: What If the Slope Changes? Compare two demand curves: \[Q_1 = 100 - 2P \quad \text{and} \quad Q_2 = 100 - 8P\] P <- seq(1, 12, by = 0.5) Q1 <- 100 - 2 * P Q2 <- 100 - 8 * P E1 <- (-2) * (P / Q1) E2 <- (-8) * (P / Q2) plot(P, Q1, type = "l", lwd = 2, ylim = c(0, 100), xlab = "Price", ylab = "Quantity", main = "What If the Slope of Demand Changes?") lines(P, Q2, lwd = 2, lty = 2) legend("topright", legend = c("Q1 = 100 - 2P", "Q2 = 100 - 8P"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2) plot(P, E1, type = "l", lwd = 2, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Elasticity", main = "Elasticity Under Different Demand Slopes") lines(P, E2, lwd = 2, lty = 2) legend("bottomleft", legend = c("E1 (slope = -2)", "E2 (slope = -8)"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2) Notes: Q2 <- 100 - 8 * P — quantity falls by 8 when price rises by 1 (more responsive). E2 <- (-8) * (P / Q2) — uses slope \(-8\) directly in the elasticity formula. lines(P, Q2, lwd = 2, lty = 2) — adds a second line to the existing graph. lty = 2 gives a dashed style to distinguish the curves. ylim = c(0, 100) — controls the vertical range so both curves fit on the same graph. Economic interpretation: A larger absolute slope means quantity changes more strongly when price changes — but elasticity still also depends on the \(P/Q\) ratio, so both slope and location on the curve matter. 15.5.2 Example B: What If the Intercept Changes? Compare: \[Q_1 = 100 - 5P \quad \text{and} \quad Q_2 = 120 - 5P\] These have the same slope but different intercepts — a parallel shift in demand. P <- seq(1, 18, by = 0.5) Q1 <- 100 - 5 * P Q2 <- 120 - 5 * P E1 <- (-5) * (P / Q1) E2 <- (-5) * (P / Q2) plot(P, Q1, type = "l", lwd = 2, ylim = c(0, 120), xlab = "Price", ylab = "Quantity", main = "What If Demand Shifts Outward?") lines(P, Q2, lwd = 2, lty = 2) legend("topright", legend = c("Q1 (intercept = 100)", "Q2 (intercept = 120)"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2) plot(P, E1, type = "l", lwd = 2, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Elasticity", main = "Elasticity When the Demand Intercept Changes") lines(P, E2, lwd = 2, lty = 2) legend("bottomleft", legend = c("E1 (intercept = 100)", "E2 (intercept = 120)"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2) Economics interpretation: An outward shift changes quantity at each price. Since elasticity includes \(Q\) in the denominator: \[E = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}\] the same slope can produce different elasticities if quantity differs. Two demand curves can have the same slope and still have different elasticities. 15.6 Elasticity When a Supply Curve Is Given Suppose supply is \(Q = 10 + 3P\). Then \(dQ/dP = 3\) and: \[E_s = 3 \cdot \frac{P}{Q}\] P <- seq(1, 20, by = 0.5) Q <- 10 + 3 * P elasticity <- 3 * (P / Q) plot(P, Q, type = "l", lwd = 2, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Quantity Supplied", main = "Supply Curve") plot(P, elasticity, type = "l", lwd = 2, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Price Elasticity of Supply", main = "Elasticity Along the Supply Curve") The code structure is exactly like the demand example, but now the derivative is positive — because supply curves slope upward. Responsiveness can still vary along the supply curve even when the slope is constant.

16 Part II. Comparative Statics 16.1 Example 1: Market Equilibrium When Demand Shifts 16.2 Example 2: Wage Changes and Labor Demand 16.3 Example 3: Productivity Changes 16.4 Example 4: Rental Rate of Capital and Optimal Inputs

16 Part II. Comparative Statics Comparative statics asks: If one parameter changes, what happens to the economic outcome? 16.1 Example 1: Market Equilibrium When Demand Shifts Demand: \(Q_d = a - 2P\)   Supply: \(Q_s = 10 + P\) We vary the demand intercept \(a\) and track equilibrium price and quantity. a_values <- seq(40, 80, by = 5) P_eq <- numeric(length(a_values)) Q_eq <- numeric(length(a_values)) for (i in seq_along(a_values)) { a <- a_values[i] P_eq[i] <- (a - 10) / 3 Q_eq[i] <- 10 + P_eq[i] } plot(a_values, P_eq, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Demand Intercept (a)", ylab = "Equilibrium Price", main = "Comparative Statics: Demand Shift and Price") plot(a_values, Q_eq, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Demand Intercept (a)", ylab = "Equilibrium Quantity", main = "Comparative Statics: Demand Shift and Quantity") Notes: a_values <- seq(40, 80, by = 5) — creates different demand scenarios (low, medium, high). P_eq <- numeric(length(a_values)) — creates an empty storage vector; without this, computed values disappear after each loop step. for (i in seq_along(a_values)) — loops through each demand intercept. seq_along() is safer than manually specifying the number of steps. P_eq[i] <- (a - 10) / 3 — solves \(a - 2P = 10 + P\) for equilibrium price. type = "b" — shows both points and connecting lines, revealing the trend across discrete cases. Economic interpretation: As demand shifts outward, both equilibrium price and quantity rise — consistent with standard supply-demand analysis. 16.2 Example 2: Wage Changes and Labor Demand Suppose a firm’s labor demand is \(L = \frac{80}{w}\). w_grid <- seq(1, 10, by = 0.5) L_star <- 80 / w_grid plot(w_grid, L_star, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Wage", ylab = "Labor Demand", main = "Comparative Statics: Wage and Labor Demand") Economics meaning: Higher wages make labor more expensive, so the firm hires less. L_star <- 80 / w_grid uses R’s vectorization — if wage is 1, \(L = 80\); if wage is 2, \(L = 40\); if wage is 10, \(L = 8\). 16.3 Example 3: Productivity Changes Production function: \(Q = AK^{0.4}L^{0.6}\). Hold \(K\) and \(L\) fixed, vary \(A\). K <- 16 L <- 25 A_values <- seq(1, 5, by = 0.25) Q <- numeric(length(A_values)) for (i in seq_along(A_values)) { A <- A_values[i] Q[i] <- A * K^0.4 * L^0.6 } plot(A_values, Q, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Technology Parameter A", ylab = "Output", main = "Comparative Statics: Technology and Output") Why keep \(K\) and \(L\) fixed? Comparative statics changes one thing at a time. By fixing inputs, we isolate the effect of technology alone. 16.4 Example 4: Rental Rate of Capital and Optimal Inputs Suppose \(Q = AK^{0.5}L^{0.5}\), with \(\bar{Q} = 20\), \(A = 2\), \(w = 4\). Vary the rental rate \(r\). set.seed(123) A <- 2 Qbar <- 20 w <- 4 r_grid <- seq(1, 10, by = 1) K_star <- numeric(length(r_grid)) L_star <- numeric(length(r_grid)) cost_star <- numeric(length(r_grid)) for (i in seq_along(r_grid)) { r <- r_grid[i] K_star[i] <- 10 / sqrt(r) L_star[i] <- 10 * sqrt(r) / w cost_star[i] <- r * K_star[i] + w * L_star[i] } plot(r_grid, K_star, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Rental Rate of Capital", ylab = "Capital Choice", main = "Comparative Statics: r and K*") plot(r_grid, L_star, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Rental Rate of Capital", ylab = "Labor Choice", main = "Comparative Statics: r and L*") plot(r_grid, cost_star, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Rental Rate of Capital", ylab = "Total Cost", main = "Comparative Statics: r and Cost") Notes: set.seed(123) — fixes the random seed for reproducibility whenever random number generation is used. K_star[i] <- 10 / sqrt(r) — as capital becomes more expensive, the firm uses less capital. (sqrt(r) = square root of r; dividing by a larger number makes \(K^*\) smaller.) L_star[i] <- 10 * sqrt(r) / w — labor moves in the opposite direction, illustrating input substitution. cost_star[i] <- r * K_star[i] + w * L_star[i] — total cost = spending on capital + spending on labor.
part-iii.-first-order-differential-equations-and-euler-method.html
17 Part III. First-Order Differential Equations and Euler Method 17.1 From Static Change to Change Over Time 17.2 The Euler Method

17 Part III. First-Order Differential Equations and Euler Method 17.1 From Static Change to Change Over Time Suppose capital evolves according to: \[\frac{dK}{dt} = sK^\alpha - \delta K\] This is a dynamic differential equation — it tells us the rate at which capital changes over time. \(sK^\alpha\): investment or accumulation \(\delta K\): depreciation 17.2 The Euler Method The Euler method approximates the differential equation with discrete steps: \[K_{t+1} = K_t + \Delta t \left( sK_t^\alpha - \delta K_t \right)\] This means: start from the current value → calculate the current rate of change → move forward by a small time step → repeat. set.seed(123) s <- sample(1:5, 1) alpha <- runif(1, 0.2, 0.9) delta <- sample(1:5, 1) dt <- 0.01 T <- 300 K0_values <- seq(1, 10, by = 2) plot(NULL, xlim = c(1, T), ylim = c(0, 15), xlab = "Time Step", ylab = "Capital", main = "Euler Simulation of Capital Dynamics") for (K0 in K0_values) { K <- numeric(T) K[1] <- K0 for (t in 1:(T - 1)) { K[t + 1] <- K[t] + dt * (s * K[t]^alpha - delta * K[t]) } lines(1:T, K, lwd = 2) } Notes: s <- sample(1:5, 1) — chooses one integer between 1 and 5 as the savings/investment parameter. alpha <- runif(1, 0.2, 0.9) — draws a random number between 0.2 and 0.9; values \(\in (0,1)\) imply diminishing returns. dt <- 0.01 — a smaller time step gives a finer, more accurate approximation. T <- 300 — the number of discrete steps (not continuous time). K0_values <- seq(1, 10, by = 2) — different starting values of capital, letting us ask: Do economies converge to the same long-run outcome? plot(NULL, ...) — creates an empty graph window; then lines() adds each trajectory. K[1] <- K0 — sets the initial condition (essential in any differential equation). for (t in 1:(T - 1)) — stops at \(T-1\) because updating \(K[t+1]\) requires \(t < T\). The core Euler update: K[t]^alpha — current capital raised to power alpha (^ = exponent in R) s * K[t]^alpha - delta * K[t] — net change in capital dt * (...) — scales by the time step K[t] + ... — adds the small change to current capital Economic interpretation: Look for whether paths rise or fall, whether different starting points converge to a common steady state, and whether the system appears stable or unstable.
part-iv.-integral-calculus-in-economics.html
18 Part IV. Integral Calculus in Economics 18.1 Consumer Surplus from an Inverse Demand Curve

18 Part IV. Integral Calculus in Economics Integration is about accumulation. In economics, it often means: total surplus total revenue from a marginal schedule total cost from a marginal cost schedule 18.1 Consumer Surplus from an Inverse Demand Curve Suppose inverse demand is \(P(Q) = 20 - Q\) and market price is 5. Consumer surplus is the area between the demand curve and the price line up to the purchased quantity. Q <- seq(0, 20, by = 0.1) P <- 20 - Q plot(Q, P, type = "l", lwd = 2, xlab = "Quantity", ylab = "Price", main = "Inverse Demand Curve and Consumer Surplus") abline(h = 5, lty = 2, lwd = 2) # Shade the consumer surplus region Q_shade <- seq(0, 15, by = 0.1) P_shade <- 20 - Q_shade polygon(c(Q_shade, rev(Q_shade)), c(P_shade, rep(5, length(Q_shade))), col = rgb(0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 0.2), border = NA) legend("topright", legend = c("Inverse Demand", "Market Price = 5", "Consumer Surplus"), lty = c(1, 2, NA), lwd = c(2, 2, NA), fill = c(NA, NA, rgb(0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 0.2)), border = c(NA, NA, "black")) demand <- function(Q) { 20 - Q } CS <- integrate(function(Q) demand(Q) - 5, lower = 0, upper = 15) CS ## 112.5 with absolute error < 0.0000000000012 Notes: Q <- seq(0, 20, by = 0.1) — many points for a smooth inverse demand curve. P <- 20 - Q — inverse demand: price as a function of quantity. abline(h = 5, lty = 2, lwd = 2) — draws a horizontal reference line at the market price (the price consumers actually pay). demand <- function(Q) { 20 - Q } — defines a function because integrate() requires a function as input. integrate(function(Q) demand(Q) - 5, lower = 0, upper = 15): demand(Q) - 5 — the vertical gap between willingness to pay and actual price (surplus per unit) lower = 0, upper = 15 — integration bounds. Why 15? Because \(5 = 20 - Q \Rightarrow Q = 15\). The integral adds up all those small gaps from 0 to 15, producing total consumer surplus.
part-v.-elasticity-when-demand-and-supply-schedules-are-given.html
19 Part V. Elasticity When Demand and Supply Schedules Are Given 19.1 Price Elasticity from a Demand Schedule 19.2 Price Elasticity from a Supply Schedule 19.3 Combined Classroom Example:

19 Part V. Elasticity When Demand and Supply Schedules Are Given Sometimes economists are not given an equation like \(Q = 100 - 5P\) and are instead given a schedule — a table of prices and quantities. In that case, elasticity is calculated between two points in the schedule. The most common method is arc elasticity. 19.1 Price Elasticity from a Demand Schedule 19.1.1 Economic Idea Suppose we are given the following demand schedule: Price Quantity Demanded 2 100 3 80 4 65 5 50 6 40 We want to measure how responsive demand is as price changes from one row to the next. 19.1.2 Arc Elasticity Formula \[E_d = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{P_{avg}}{Q_{avg}}\] where: \[\Delta Q = Q_2 - Q_1, \quad \Delta P = P_2 - P_1, \quad P_{avg} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2}, \quad Q_{avg} = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2}{2}\] We use averages because the midpoint method is more balanced than using only the starting or ending value. 19.1.3 Manual Calculation for the First Interval Moving from Price = 2, Quantity = 100 to Price = 3, Quantity = 80: \[\Delta Q = 80 - 100 = -20, \quad \Delta P = 3 - 2 = 1, \quad P_{avg} = 2.5, \quad Q_{avg} = 90\] \[E_d = \frac{-20}{1} \cdot \frac{2.5}{90} = -0.556\] Interpretation: The negative sign reflects the law of demand. Because the absolute value is less than 1, demand is inelastic over this interval. 19.1.4 R Code for a Demand Schedule price_d <- c(2, 3, 4, 5, 6) quantity_d <- c(100, 80, 65, 50, 40) elasticity_d <- numeric(length(price_d) - 1) for (i in 1:(length(price_d) - 1)) { dQ <- quantity_d[i + 1] - quantity_d[i] dP <- price_d[i + 1] - price_d[i] Qavg <- (quantity_d[i + 1] + quantity_d[i]) / 2 Pavg <- (price_d[i + 1] + price_d[i]) / 2 elasticity_d[i] <- (dQ / dP) * (Pavg / Qavg) } price_mid_d <- (price_d[-1] + price_d[-length(price_d)]) / 2 demand_results <- data.frame( price_midpoint = price_mid_d, elasticity_demand = round(elasticity_d, 3) ) print(demand_results) ## price_midpoint elasticity_demand ## 1 2.5 -0.556 ## 2 3.5 -0.724 ## 3 4.5 -1.174 ## 4 5.5 -1.222 Notes: elasticity_d <- numeric(length(price_d) - 1) — creates storage for 4 elasticity values (5 rows → 4 intervals). for (i in 1:(length(price_d) - 1)) — loops through each interval: row 1→2, 2→3, 3→4, 4→5. dQ <- quantity_d[i + 1] - quantity_d[i] — change in quantity between adjacent rows. dP <- price_d[i + 1] - price_d[i] — change in price between adjacent rows. elasticity_d[i] <- (dQ / dP) * (Pavg / Qavg) — applies the arc elasticity formula; [i] places the result in the correct slot of the storage vector. price_d[-1] means “all elements except the first”; price_d[-length(price_d)] means “all elements except the last” — combining them gives the midpoint of each interval. 19.1.5 Plot plot(price_d, quantity_d, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Quantity Demanded", main = "Demand Schedule") plot(price_mid_d, elasticity_d, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Price Midpoint", ylab = "Arc Elasticity of Demand", main = "Elasticity from a Demand Schedule") Economic interpretation: Elasticity may differ from interval to interval — demand can be more elastic in some price ranges and less elastic in others. Responsiveness is not automatically constant even in a simple table. 19.2 Price Elasticity from a Supply Schedule 19.2.1 Economic Idea Now suppose we are given a supply schedule: Price Quantity Supplied 2 20 3 35 4 50 5 70 6 95 The arc elasticity formula is the same, but now \(Q\) means quantity supplied. Because supply usually slopes upward, elasticity is usually positive. 19.2.2 Manual Calculation for the First Interval Moving from Price = 2, Quantity Supplied = 20 to Price = 3, Quantity Supplied = 35: \[\Delta Q = 15, \quad \Delta P = 1, \quad P_{avg} = 2.5, \quad Q_{avg} = 27.5\] \[E_s = \frac{15}{1} \cdot \frac{2.5}{27.5} = 1.364\] Interpretation: Because elasticity is greater than 1, supply is elastic over this interval — producers respond strongly to price changes. 19.2.3 R Code for a Supply Schedule price_s <- c(2, 3, 4, 5, 6) quantity_s <- c(20, 35, 50, 70, 95) elasticity_s <- numeric(length(price_s) - 1) for (i in 1:(length(price_s) - 1)) { dQ <- quantity_s[i + 1] - quantity_s[i] dP <- price_s[i + 1] - price_s[i] Qavg <- (quantity_s[i + 1] + quantity_s[i]) / 2 Pavg <- (price_s[i + 1] + price_s[i]) / 2 elasticity_s[i] <- (dQ / dP) * (Pavg / Qavg) } price_mid_s <- (price_s[-1] + price_s[-length(price_s)]) / 2 supply_results <- data.frame( price_midpoint = price_mid_s, elasticity_supply = round(elasticity_s, 3) ) print(supply_results) ## price_midpoint elasticity_supply ## 1 2.5 1.364 ## 2 3.5 1.235 ## 3 4.5 1.500 ## 4 5.5 1.667 The code structure is exactly like the demand schedule, but quantities come from the supply table. The sign and economic interpretation differ — supply slopes upward, so elasticity is positive. 19.2.4 Plot plot(price_s, quantity_s, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Quantity Supplied", main = "Supply Schedule") plot(price_mid_s, elasticity_s, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Price Midpoint", ylab = "Arc Elasticity of Supply", main = "Elasticity from a Supply Schedule") 19.3 Combined Classroom Example: # --- Demand schedule --- price_d <- c(2, 3, 4, 5, 6) quantity_d <- c(100, 80, 65, 50, 40) elasticity_d <- numeric(length(price_d) - 1) for (i in 1:(length(price_d) - 1)) { dQ <- quantity_d[i + 1] - quantity_d[i] dP <- price_d[i + 1] - price_d[i] Qavg <- (quantity_d[i + 1] + quantity_d[i]) / 2 Pavg <- (price_d[i + 1] + price_d[i]) / 2 elasticity_d[i] <- (dQ / dP) * (Pavg / Qavg) } price_mid_d <- (price_d[-1] + price_d[-length(price_d)]) / 2 # --- Supply schedule --- price_s <- c(2, 3, 4, 5, 6) quantity_s <- c(20, 35, 50, 70, 95) elasticity_s <- numeric(length(price_s) - 1) for (i in 1:(length(price_s) - 1)) { dQ <- quantity_s[i + 1] - quantity_s[i] dP <- price_s[i + 1] - price_s[i] Qavg <- (quantity_s[i + 1] + quantity_s[i]) / 2 Pavg <- (price_s[i + 1] + price_s[i]) / 2 elasticity_s[i] <- (dQ / dP) * (Pavg / Qavg) } price_mid_s <- (price_s[-1] + price_s[-length(price_s)]) / 2 # --- Results tables --- demand_results <- data.frame( price_midpoint = price_mid_d, elasticity_demand = round(elasticity_d, 3) ) supply_results <- data.frame( price_midpoint = price_mid_s, elasticity_supply = round(elasticity_s, 3) ) print(demand_results) ## price_midpoint elasticity_demand ## 1 2.5 -0.556 ## 2 3.5 -0.724 ## 3 4.5 -1.174 ## 4 5.5 -1.222 print(supply_results) ## price_midpoint elasticity_supply ## 1 2.5 1.364 ## 2 3.5 1.235 ## 3 4.5 1.500 ## 4 5.5 1.667 # --- Four graphs --- par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(price_d, quantity_d, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Quantity Demanded", main = "Demand Schedule") plot(price_mid_d, elasticity_d, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Price Midpoint", ylab = "Demand Elasticity", main = "Elasticity from Demand Schedule") plot(price_s, quantity_s, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Price", ylab = "Quantity Supplied", main = "Supply Schedule") plot(price_mid_s, elasticity_s, type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "Price Midpoint", ylab = "Supply Elasticity", main = "Elasticity from Supply Schedule") par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
